title: Function declarations, expressions and arrows

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Function declarations, expressions and arrows; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. canonicalExplanation.summary: Function declarations, expressions, arrows, methods, and classes differ in hoisting, this, arguments, constructibility, and naming.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An arrow callback reads surrounding this intentionally without rebinding it. Your goal is to implement equivalent callable behavior and explain where arrow semantics differ. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Values, types and expressions; pf abstraction calls; Loops and iterable traversal; pf abstraction functions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Function declarations, expressions, arrows, methods, and classes differ in hoisting, this, arguments, constructibility, and naming. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Arrow functions capture lexical this and cannot be constructors, while methods receive this from the call form. This draft therefore rejects: Arrow functions are shorter syntax for every function and can always replace methods or constructors.

includes: Choose callable forms while understanding hoisting, lexical this and expression returns.
Implement equivalent callable behavior and explain where arrow semantics differ.
excludes: Arrow functions capture lexical this and cannot be constructors, while methods receive this from the call form.

outcomes: Implement equivalent callable behavior and explain where arrow semantics differ.

examples.title: Function declarations, expressions and arrows: concrete case
examples.situation: An arrow callback reads surrounding this intentionally without rebinding it.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: An arrow callback reads surrounding this intentionally without rebinding it.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Arrow functions have lexical this and are not constructible; choose a form from call semantics, not length.
